affected_groups: Teachers and staff in general schools and surviving urban gimnáziumok
Families and pupils facing displacement, longer commutes or loss of local school options
Rural and middle-SES communities whose local gimnázium capacity is at risk
Disadvantaged (low-SES) applicants facing intensified competition for shrinking places
Church and foundation maintainers positioned to absorb displaced demand
Employers and the economy dependent on the STEM/digital talent pipeline
content.decision_question: Equity and system stability versus efficiency-through-contraction: shrinking capacity without funding floors or anti-sorting rules pursues a supply-side efficiency goal at the expense of equity, predictability and viability for those left in or displaced from the state sector
content.evidence_boundary: Contested/unknown: whether church and foundation maintainers would in fact expand to offset state-sector contraction is a behavioural prediction without settled evidence in this record; the mechanical link between contraction and rural closure, class-size growth and SES-skew increase is plausible and consistent with cited displacement/rejection-rate figures but the net system-wide magnitude and whether compensatory funding could offset it are not empirically resolved here
content.tension: Shrinking state-sector gimnázium capacity via score thresholds without funding commitments or anti-sorting rules simply accelerates consolidation, worsens rural school viability, intensifies competition for remaining places, and does not address—may worsen—SES-based exclusion.
content.title: Decision tension A4: S2
value_poles: efficiency-through-contraction: shrinking capacity without funding floors or anti-sorting rules pursues a supply-side efficiency goal at the expense of equity, predictability and viability for those left in or displaced from the state sector
